title: description
text:
More details on how researchers can use BioHUG data will be available here in the summer 2026. Below is some early information:
{{< image src="images/BioHUG_2026_outline.png" alt="BioHUG outline" width="300px" align="center" >}}






NoneWhat are the inclusion and exclustion criteria for BioHUG ?





BioHUG consists of adult HUG patients who have signed the general consent as and adult at any point since 2017, not subsequently withdrawn that consent, and have had a routine blood test since February 2025.









NoneCan you prioritise samples from patients in my research study ?





Yes. If the patients in your research study have signed the general consent, and not subsequently withdrawn that consent, we can prioritise them for DNA extraction. Bear in mind that the DNA banking step relies on a patient having a separate blood test as part of their routine care.









NoneWill I need additional ethical approval to use BioHUG ?





Yes. You will need to apply for ethical approval for your study. BioHUG can do two things to help 1. Help design your study. For example if you wish to start with patients who already have a banked DNA sample, or genome wide association data, we can provide meta data (for example summaries of numbers of patients) 2. If you have a group of patients in your study and you wish to reuse genetic data from those who are already in BioHUG.









NoneCan I collect more than one sample from the same patient ?





Yes if you let us know we can prioritise collection of 2 or more samples from the same patient. We do this securely in the HUG system, but you need to inform us because the default is to only collect one DNA sample per patient.









NoneHow representative is BioHUG ?





BioHUG is very representative of the background Geneva population. No new blood sample or research questions or tests are performed. This lowers the barriers to participation. We estimate that 25% of participants are of non-European genetic ancestry.









NoneAre samples other than DNA available ?





As of March 2026, no. Since beginning DNA biobanking in February 2025, BioHUG has collected only DNA samples. However, it may be possible later in 2026 or 2027 to bank serum and plasma samples.









NoneHow much does it cost ?





Nothing for DNA banking. There is no cost to researchers to bank DNA. Funding is in place to extract DNA from 20,000 HUG patients who have signed the general consent. This initial collection will be complete in early 2027. We will also have genome wide genetic data, from the GSAMD illumina array available from 5000 in summer 2026. In the interests of data security the individual level data will only be available to a limited set of trained researchers. We will be able to provide summary level data at no cost.
Genetic data on additional samples, and banking of serum and plasma is subject to further funding, but we will be able to do this in a very cost efficient way. For more information please contact the PI of the study Prof. Timothy Frayling at timothy.frayling@unige.ch



:::






NoneIs BioHUG accredited with the Swiss Biobanking platform ?





Yes. BioHUG has Swiss Biobanking Platform accreditation. More details here.

text:
More details on how researchers can use BioHUG data will be available here in the summer 2026. Below is some early information:
{{< image src="images/BioHUG_2026_outline.png" alt="BioHUG outline" width="300px" align="center" >}}






NoneWhat are the inclusion and exclustion criteria for BioHUG ?

:::






NoneIs BioHUG accredited with the Swiss Biobanking platform ?





Yes. BioHUG has Swiss Biobanking Platform accreditation. More details here.

title: FAQs
text:
NoneHow can DNA be collected from me and used for research ?





When you have a routine blood test, for example to check your cholesterol levels or how well your kidneys are functioning, there is often some blood left over. This unused blood is usually thrown in the trash after a few days. For BioHUG, we intercept these blood samples, if you have signed the general consent, and extract DNA in laboratories at the HUG.  
In early 2026, over 150,000 Geneva residents have signed the general consent giving permission for their medical data and samples to be reused for research. BioHUG is aiming to bank DNA from 20,000 of these Geneva residents by early 2027. This will make BioHUG one of the largest biomedical research projects in Europe from a single hospital.









NoneWho can participate ?





Anyone who has signed the general consent could be part of BioHUG. The general consent form can be found here.









NoneHow does BioHUG handle my spare blood ?





Although the samples come from patients the sample is de-identified. This means that each sample is given a special code that is separate from the patient ID in the HUG. Researchers only see this special code and not the patient ID. BioHUG takes patient privacy very seriously and no names, dates of birth or addresses are ever available to researchers. For each researcher using BioHUG, the de-identified special code is linked to a limited set of medical information that is relevant to that researcher. For example a researcher wishing to study Diabetes will only use data relevant to diabetes.









NoneWill someone let me know if something is found in my left over sample ?





No. We do not perform the tests in a clinical laboratory and to clinical standards. However, we hope that , by using your DNA and other samples from your blood, that new, improved treatments will be possible.









NoneWhat if I do not want my left over sample used in this way ?





We understand that some people will not want their sample used in this way. If you do not want your sample used in this way, or you change your mind in the future, you can contact the general consent team at HUG: aider-la-recherche@hcuge.ch or telephone: 022 372 91 57. If you choose to withdraw, our medical record system will remember this choice and your leftover samples and its resultant information will not be used for new BioHUG-related research. Your doctors do not know if you participate in BioHUG. You will recieve the same care whether or not your samples are in BioHUG.

title: Consent
text: If you have signed the general consent in the past your samples could be part of BioHUG. This consent allows researchers to reuse data and samples for research, with strict privacy protection. The general consent form can be found here.

title: What is BioHUG ?
text:
NoneWhat is BioHUG ?





BioHUG is the HUG’s collection, or “bank” of deidentified samples, which includes DNA. The samples come from patients who have signed the general consent that means they have given permission to researchers to reuse their medical data and samples for research. Patients can change their decision at anytime by contacting: aider-la-recherche@hcuge.ch or telephone: 022 372 91 57 .
DNA is present in almost all your cells and represents the plans or “map” of how to build a human body and how to keep it functioning well. Changes to the plans exist in the form of small changes to the DNA inherited from your parents and it is these changes that influence your hair and eye colour, your height and weight, your risk of disease and how well you respond to treatments. Researchers are using these samples to understand why certain diseases occur in certain people or why a drug works better in some people than others. This approach to understanding disease, using human data and DNA samples, is extremely important for medical research and is used extensively to search for new treatments and side effects throughout the world.









NoneHow does it work ?





When you have a routine blood test, for example to check your cholesterol levels or how well your kidneys are functioning, there is often some blood left over. This unused blood is usually thrown in the trash after a few days. For BioHUG, instead of wasting the spare blood, we reuse it to extract DNA in laboratories at the HUG for research projects. We may also store the blood for other research applications.
In early 2026, over 150,000 Geneva residents have signed the general consent giving permission for their medical data and samples to be reused for research. BioHUG is aiming to bank DNA from 20,000 of these Geneva residents by early 2027. This will make BioHUG one of the largest biomedical research projects in Europe from a single hospital.









NoneWhat are the objectives of BioHUG ?





BioHUG is not a specific research project. Instead its aim is to provide the foundations for new research projects. These research projects could include many different diseases and responses to treatment and will usually include linking DNA information to medical information to understand in more detail the disease. An example of one of the first research project arising from BioHUG is “CALIBRATE”. Link.









NoneHow do I sign up for BioHUG ? ?





We do not need you to sign up again to the study if you have already signed the general consent. This means no new blood samples or information is required.









NoneWhat are the benefits to patients ?





There are no immediate direct benefits to individual patients and we cannot return information to you as the DNA analyses are not performed by clinicians, but by researchers. Instead, contributing to BioHUG is a contribution to finding new links between genes and disease. These new links can then be tested in separate studies. Some other hospitals around the world have very similar banks of DNA in 10,000s of patients and these are being used to make important discoveries that are then tested further. For example, a recent study using several similar DNA “banks” found a link between changes in a certain gene and missed diagnoses of Diabetes Diabetes.
